title: Et Tu Certifications Robustness Certificates Yield Better Adversarial Examples
abstract: In guaranteeing the absence of adversarial examples in an instance’s neighbourhood, certification mechanisms play an important role in demonstrating neural net robustness. In this paper, we ask if these certifications can compromise the very models they help to protect? Our new Certification Aware Attack exploits certifications to produce computationally efficient norm-minimising adversarial examples 74% more often than comparable attacks, while reducing the median perturbation norm by more than 10%. While these attacks can be used to assess the tightness of certification bounds, they also highlight that releasing certifications can paradoxically reduce security.
impact: This work explores a heretofore undiscovered security risk associated with publishing the certifications associated with certifiable robust mechanisms. While such attacks can induce negative societal consequences, there is a clear consensus within the computer security community that responsible disclosure of attacks is a crucial part of improving systemic security. Training-time, test-time and backdoor attacks are always published in the public domain, and the risk associated with the publication of our new attack should be viewed within this context. The new attack vector contained within this work allows for a fundamental reevaluation of the security associated with systems designed to demonstrate the resistance of machine learning models to adversarial manipulation, and provides a new mechanism that can be used to better study the tightness of the bounds provided by robustness mechanisms.

query_input.scenario: LLM content moderation and safety alignment systems
query_input.issue: Adversarial prompts evade certified LLM safety guardrails, enabling generation of harmful content.
hazard: Certification-aware adversarial examples exploit tightness weaknesses in robustness certification bounds to achieve evasion with minimized perturbation norm.
exposure: Users of LLM-powered assistants encountering harmful generated content.

hipporag_result.vulnerability: False sense of security from certification guarantees reduces vigilance, leaving users unprotected against certification-exploiting adversarial prompts.
hipporag_result.impact: Users encounter harmful generated content, leading to psychological harm, spread of misinformation, and erosion of trust in AI assistants.
hipporag_result.dose_response: Certification-aware adversarial examples exploiting bound tightness weaknesses, through users of LLM-powered assistants encountering harmful generated content, trigger a false sense of security that reduces vigilance, leaving users unprotected, leading to harmful generated content, psychological harm, misinformation, and erosion of trust.

dose_response: Certification-aware adversarial examples with minimized perturbations evade LLM safety guardrails, which exposes users to harmful content, leading to psychological harm and legal liability.
vulnerability: Safety guardrails overfit to standard prompts, leaving gaps for novel adversarial patterns.
User over-trust in LLM safety branding amplifies harm when guardrails fail.
Certification methods lack scalability across under-resourced languages, creating blind spots.
impact: Proliferation of harmful content causing psychological harm to users and third parties.
Legal liability and fines for platform operators under content safety laws.
Loss of consumer trust, leading to reduced adoption and market share.
